GCURBRENT AFFAIRS

Verily, “testimonials’’ will never cease. There is
one being engineereed now for the man Coote, Justico
of the Peace, and “hero’’ in the great “liberty””
movement which freed the Carnthall “National
School, in the County of Tyrone, of the dangerous
“Tdolatrous’ sewing instructress. Two D.L.’s and a Pro-

 testant Rector are engaged in sending round the hat for
the man Coote. Mr. Anketell Moutray, D.L., J.P.,
is “chairman of the man Coote testimonial, and Mr.
John Carmichael-Ferral, D.L., is treasurer; Mr. J.
‘Winter, B.A., Rector, Augher, is hon. sec. We take
the following from the touting circular sent out on be-
} f the man Coote by these liberal-minded ”” gen-

»

There is a very widespread feeling amongst,

‘Wm.

‘the physically weak were

Clogher, should not be permitted to bear the burden
alone of the very serious costs involved in fighting this
case. The decision of the Court of Appeal is one which
protects the liberty of action of every loyalist in this
country, and very few men would have had the courage
to fight such an expensive case alone, especially as it is
known that several revolutionary organisations were
opposed to Mr. Coote, and had conspired to accomplish
his ruin by every invention in their power.”” The end
of the tout is, perhaps, the most brazen part of it:—
“ Although Mr. Coote has got a decree against the
plaintiff he cannot realise the costs, and as he has been
out of pocket something like £500, the undersigned
have been appointed to make this appeal, and solicit

_subscriptions to this worthy object, feeling certain that

the loyalists of this country will show by their response
that they are willing to come to the aid of a man who
endeavours to arrest the encroachments of any combina-
tion on the liberties of the subject.”” Certainly, “ the
encroachments of any combination on the liberties of the
subject ”’ is not bad considering the circumstances!
Perhaps the appointment of Miss Sweeny, an “ Idola-
trous ”’ sewing instructress, to the Carnthall School
was a menace to the integrity of the Empire. Captain
Shawe-Taylor ought to go up to Carnthall, and orate
about the “ proffered hand of friendship.”’

Mr. Godfrey Fetherstonhaugh, K.C., a Unionist can-
didate for North Fermanagh, has quite an interesting
collection of orators in his support. A particular pair
of them are specially interesting; they are the man
Coote, of Carnthall, and James Campbell, ex-Attorney-
G:neral. They are well matched, anyway. We read
in a local sheet that “ Mr. William Coote, J.P., will
come from South Tyrone, and he is such a true man he
will be warmly welcomed in Fermanagh.”’

The Government shut the public-houses on Christ-
mas Day, but they left the “bona’’ door open. . We
have no doubt the Rathfarnham, Blackrock, and Clon-
tarf trams did well in consequence. It is, on the face of
it, absurd to nave left the “bona’’ door open, and the
people of Dublin have to congratulate themselves that
their city was not the scene of drunkenness which, by
all accounts, characterised Belfast, where the ‘ civic’*
virtue comes from. A local daily paper, the Irisk
News, says in a leading article:—“ All to whom the
good name of Belfast is a matter of concern, as it should
be to every citizen, will feel pained and disgusted at the
disgraceful scenes of drunkenness which marked Christ-
mas Day in this city.”” Our contemporary also re-
marks:—* Unable to procure intoxicating liquor in
the city, those who think drunkenness necessary to en-
joyment travelled out to the suburbs, where licensed
premises were open to bona fide travellers, and by this
simple device they succeeded in getting as much liquor
as they could hold, and a good deal more. Some of the
suburban roads, we understand, were strewn with the
prostrate bodies of degraded persons of this type.” In
the news columns of the same paper we read :—‘ Men,
women, and even children, were to be met with lying .

.along the roads and side walks in the last stages of hope-

less intoxication, while those who were able to. keep
their feet engaged in brutal bouts of fisticuffs. Even
not wanting amongst the
motley crowd, the ‘lame, the blind, and the halt’ ap-
pearing for the nonce to have forsaken the city, and
joined in the rush for the suburban pubs. The out-
going trams were besieged, but on the return journeys
they were practically empty, because the conductors
very wisely refused to allow any with the signs of dri;
to board the cars. Several of the men were subjecte
to violent treatment in consequence, but they discharg:
their duties with tact and discretion notwithstanding

That. a’ pretty class: of suburban publicans they
ust have in the vicinity of Belfast. How is it that
_these “‘hona ’’ Bung spongers on Christmas Day" were
ot all looked after by the police?

_Those interested in the Town Tenants’ Movement
will feel great sympathy with Mr. Jeremiah Cronin, of
‘Mqcroom, Co. Cork, on the death of his daughter at
quite an early age. Mr. Cronin has been called the
father of the Town Tenants’ Movement. It was only
recently he was principal speaker at Courtmacsherry

" ab the establishment of a branch in that town. Court-
‘wuacsherry appears to be in need of a Town Tenants’
Organisation.  Lord Shannon let parcels of land at
10s. each, and now that buildings have been ecrected
apd the leases have fallen in, his lordship, though he
did not sow, nor neither did he spin, wants to extract
50s. for these plots!  According to a speaker at the
meeting the land is less value to-day than when the
original leases were granted. Now that the tenants
have given hostages to fortune, as it were, by placing
building on these sites, Lord Shannon thinks he can
multiply the rental by five!

“It was decided that all invitations should emanate
from the committee, and that the names of all ladies
and gentlemen who wished to attend should first be
submitted to the committee, who would afterwards issue
the necessary invitation cards.’”” We take the fore-
going from a newspaper report concerning an aristo-
cratic and most exclusive ball that is shortly to come off
in  Castlebar. Lanigan’s Ball may step down in
presence of the “ Castlebar Bachelors’ Ball.”” We read
that “a largely attended meeting was held at the Im-
perial Hotel *’—they have an” “ Imperial ’ Hotel in
Irish National Castlebar—for the purpose of electing
officers and a committee to take charge of the arrange-
ments in connection with the forthcoming great aristo-
<ratic event. Verily, the West’s awake. One Dr. Bra-
bazon, one of England’s Faithful Garrison and doctor to
the local green Foresters, was in the chair on this
momentous occasion. Dr. Brabazon, we read, “is ever
willing to give his services and long experience to every-
thing that encourages sport.”” May we reverently say
“Hurroo for the Castlebar boy!”” One Mr. J. Clarke
was elected on the committee of this Castlebar social
dance inquisition that is to weigh up the merits—and
demerits—of “all ladies and gentlemen who wished: to
attend “this aristocratic function. Mr. Clarke may
be a born aristocrat; he is Secretary of the Mayo
County Council anyway. We wonder will this Secre-
tary of the Mayo County Council insist on some Irish
dances being placed upon the programme! Another
member of the committee is Dr. J. J. Hopkins. Dr.
Hopkins may oblige with a song in Irish. He may;
but we do not think that he will do so; we have reason
to fear that he is too respectable for that. Dr. Am-
brose is another member of this famous Castlebar com-
mittee, and so is Mr. J. A. O’Donnell. The latter
must be an aristocrat for he is a bank clerk; and
another member of the committee is Mr. Jack Robert-
son, a solicitor. Here indeed you have in brief a
<comedy streaked with farce of highly respectable Anglo-
Irish urban life. ;

What a flood of light would be thrown on highly
respectable Anglo-Trish urban life if a verbatim report
of the proceedings of this committee were presented to

- - -%he public! For ‘“the names of all ladies and gentle-
men who wished to attend ’’ are to be considered by
this august assembly. Will Bung be excluded? Will
_mere shop-keepers be eligible? What will the bank
_clerk say!?

Castlebar Town Hall is not good enough for this
colleotion of Castlebar elite; the ball is to be held in
the Court House. The event—one of the greatest social
~evenis of the twentieth contury so far—is to be held
‘the 7ilcldle of this month. After the ball is over we

VAl
oot

ER.

Wewonder when will the Liberal Government tackle g
the Irish University grievance ?' That University busi- =%
ness is a pressing matter, for if Ireland is ta develop
socially, industrially, and intellectually there is no time
to be lost, and we have had plenty of talk and writing
about it already. Ireland wanbs.-cngineers, industrial
pioneers, scientists, statesmen, railway managers, etc.,
for the future, and if these are not educated here they
will be imported from outside, and the country will keep
going backwards. We have quite enough intellectual
reomini who think big things of themselves because they
have passed some examinations in this country whose edu-
cational level is low ; we have so many B.A’s. that, as our
“ Christmas Editor’’ pointed out, the question is be-
coming a pressing one—What shall we do with our
B.A.’s? We have a plethora of B.L.’s, and we undfar-
stand the library of the Four Courts is a sort of city
sofa for unemployed B.L.’s, just as the base of Nel-
son’s Pillar is for unemployed of another social scale.
The Liberal Government should tackle the Irish edu-
cation problem at once. ~We want a Univer
Universities, that are capable of turning out Irishmen
competent to make a clean job of the various resources
of this country. We cannot all live by being one an-
other’s advocates in the Four Courts, and B.A.’s are
scarcely marketable at three a penny now. We want.
effective, working Irishmen rather than a job cheap-
sale lot of “learned’’ proletariat.

Right Rev. Monsignor Hallinan, of Newcastle West,
brought forward a matter of great importance at a re-
cent meeting of the Limerick County Committee of
Agriculture and Technical Instruction.  Monsignor
Hallinan proposed:—* That it is desirable in the in-
terests of efficiency in the public service that candidates
for positions under the public boards of this country
should be subjected to examination to test their fitness,
and that voting be by ballot.”” In supporting this pro-
position, Monsignor Hallinan said : — They all had ex-
perience that the fitness of a candidate was a secondary
consideration as things were worked under the present
system. Public positions had been filled not with a
view to the efliciency of the candidate, but because he
had a good back at the Board. The result was that
people who were fitted in every respect by talent and
training for the post oftentimes made no application at
all, because they found it was useless, or if they did
make application their canvass was not successful, simply
because they had not the voting powers at the public
Boards.  The consequence was that they had the
talented sons of small farmers and labourers fleeing
from the country and distinguishing themselves in
foreign lands.”” = Mr. Mitchell considered that the
Limerick County Technical Committee was “a small
place to initiate this matter.” There you have the
self-distrusting type of modern Irishman articulate!
Mr. Mitchell proceeded :— They might suggest it to
the Lord Lieutenant, the Chief Secretary, or somie one
else to fill lucrative positions in the manner proposed.
Such positions were very few at this small board and
others.”” Some Irishmen think so little of themselves
that they recoil at the very thought that they could
initiate anything. An amendment proposed by Mr.
Mitchell that the Committee do not entertain the pro-
position as it was impracticable, was carried by 10 to 5.
Even so, Monsignor Hallinan did a good stroke of work
in bringing forward the matter. Ireland needs the
right men in the right places in all her various phases
of activity if she is to attain to her greatest possible
officiency.  Public appointment jobbing is a many-
sided evil, and it is encowraging to see that the system
is being attacked.

Thav famous “ Roman Catholic Farmer of Killaloe,
who was not at home to a registered letter, and who
cost Arnott’s  Irish >’ Times so dearly, had bebter look -
to. his laurels, There is a scribe’down Skibbereen way
?’,’ho. may put him in the shade. Under $he heading

Irish Protestants and Home Rule,”’ one “ A.G.S..” in
the course of a letter which was publi X
English Z4mes, says:—Now, D

+* & cashier and paymaster for the contractors when the Jight
vailway was being built from Skibbereen to Sehull, in
West Cork, paying some £300 a week in wages. It hap-
pened that a Protestant named ‘ J.D.’ was employed at
a place called Aughadown, with his horse and cart. In
two days the whole Aughadown gang, numbering about
100, struck work; they were appealed to by the con-
tractors, engineers, and myself, with no result. On
returning to Skibbereen we were visited by two priests,
who told us we were not to employ the man ‘J.D.” or the
men would strike all along the line (16 miles). We re-
fused to give in and for two days went to Aughadown
and _loaded ‘J.D.’s’ cart ourselves with the whole gang
looking ou. When pay-day came, however, and they
found that ‘ J.D.’ got paid his full time and they werc
shorted of two days they caved in. This is an instance
of what Protestants may expeet under Home Rule.”’

Who is “ A.G.S.”" who states that he was cashier
and pay-master for the contractors who constructed the
railway referred to? We know who “J.D.” is, and
light on the identity of “ A.G.S.”” would be desirable.

“J.D.,”” the “persecuted Protestant’’ of Sourface
libel, is one Mr. Jeremiah Driscoll, of Aughadown,
Skibbereen, Co. Cork, an “ Idolator ’’ born and bred.
In the course of a letter from Mr. Jeremiah Driscoll,
that gentleman says:—“Now, sir, I am ‘J.D.’ I
was born and reared a Roman Catholic. I was then,
and am still, a Roman Catholic. I am also a Home
Ruler. I had some trouble similar to that stated by
“A.G.S.,” not on the score of religion, but on political
grounds in comnection with rent. Several Protestants
belonging to this neighbourhood, who are still alive,
were employed in the works, and neither they, nor any-
one else, were subjected to the slightest annoyance on
account of their religion.

This is quite a dramatic exposure of a class of mean
. calumny that is, unfortunately, so prevalent—perhaps
Lord Dunraven would gloss it over as an “ Electioneer-
ing dodge.”’Of course this exposure any more than that
of the Killaloe case in which the “ Zrish’’ Times got
heavily mauled and mulcted, will not stop these cal-
umnies on Irish Catholics. The calumnies will still go
merrily on. Now the chief fact concerning this policy
¥ of calumny that strikes us is the silence of the “ respect-
w1 able ”’ Protestant element in face of these base methods
of attempting to blacken the character of Irish Catholics.
What Protestant man or Protestant paper denounces
these things? They go merrily on, and so-called
“liberal’’ Protestants also go merrily on never pro-
testing or denouncing them !

We want LEADERITES living in Britain to do us—and
the old country—a bit of service during the coming
General Election. We want them to send us as many
samples of the Unionist leaflet (bearing upon matters
Irish or Catholic) as they can lay their hands on, more
especially such as seem, in their very faces, to be of a
defamatory character. Such Jeaflets we want, and also
cuttings of speeches made by the brigade of Irish

" Unionist lecturers—briefless barristers on “#hirty bob
a day *’—lately organised for service in Mr, Bull’s na-
tive land. ~The principal leaflot-issuing agencies would
be, first, the ordinary Party organisations, and next, the
Trish Unionist Alliance, and the Imperial Protestant
Federation. Cuttings from newspapers should have the
name of the paper given and its date, as well as
the place and date of the speech. With the
‘help of' these cuttings and leaflets we shall be
"in a position to show people on this side the Channel
what is being said of them on the other side. Young,

and for that matter old gentlemen, who might behave
% .with ladylike mildness at an “all-creeds-and-classes ”’
3 meeting’ in Ireland, sometimes let themselves go when
they find themselves surrounded exclusively by a “whole-
some Protestant atmosphere,”” and say things that they
wouldn’t say where an Irish reporter was known to be
present. We hope to make, during each week of the
_elections, a page or so of spicy excerpts, which, coupled

our comments, may - help to show how

gents and the leaflet-scribblers help to make. it.
vite our readers domiciled in Britain to help us in malk-
ing this ezposé.

There was a “grand concert and brilliant Xmas en-
tertainment * in the Town Hall, Tipperary, on Thurs-
day last. There is not as much as one song in Irish om
the programme. ~ Some of those whose names figure
under the items— Midget Banjo Band— White Coon,”
‘ Darkics Dream,’ ’’ also figure under the item * Irisk
reel (cight hand).””  There was a Spanish dance, &
*“ Witch dance,’” whatever that is, and a Highland fling.
We learn that Mr. Charles Dwyer, who is described om
the programme as “ the celebrated and refined come-
dian, Coon singer and dancer,”’” was hissed and booed
off the stage by portion of the audience. 0

A “variety entertaimment '’ is to be held, or was re-
cently held, in the National School House of Ballymote,
in the County of Sligo. The programme announces the
performance of a play— How’s Your Uncle? or, The
Barber of Bishopsgate.”” ~ We hear that the scene of
this dramatic master-piece is laid in Cramwell Court,
London, and, no doubt, the Ballymote “ artistes > have
been busy during the past months getting up proper
Cockney accents so as to rise to the dramatic heights of
the world-renowned master-piece. Almost, needless to
say, there is not a song in Irish on the programme; but
a “Fan dance’” is announced, and the performance
winds up with a farce entitled, “ Pat Molloy, Literary
Genius.”

Ballymote is on the hill-top of “respectability.”” A
visitor recently had occasion to purchase a small quan-
tity of note-paper whilst passing through the town. The
visitor called at one of the principal shops in the place,
and asked for three pennyworth of Irish-manufactured:
note-paper. After a search a box of Irish note-paper
was produced, but the would-be purchaser was informed:
that the establishment did not make three pennyworths
in Irish note-paper; the visitor; of course, could have
three ‘pennyworth of any other kind of note-paper.
That is a nice way to “ encourage *’ Irish manufacturel

Before the old year died, one of the props of the
everlasting hopefuls ratted. Mr. Tom Sloan, M.P., has
gone back, as it were, to the Belfast Customs” House
steps. We kept our readers well warned against men
of this type, who, however, succeeded in throwing dust
in the eyes of some of our green poeple. Mr. Sloan has
thrown over even Devolution now and with it his friend
Captain Shawe-Taylor. In the course of Mr. Sloan’s
recantation we read:—“I have learned with regret
that many Unionists, both in my own constituency and
outside of it, consider that my connection with the
Magheramorne manifesto has placed me at variance with
the policy of my Parliamentary colleagues, and of the -
Ulster Unionist party generally. As the issue mow
hefore the country is so momentous to the cause of
Unionism, I think the time has come when differences
among Ulster Unionists should be settled, so that a
anited front be presented against Home Rule, and
any insidious policy, such as Devolution, that may lead 2
to 1t."”

This ‘“independent Orangeman '’ was pretty quickly
whipped to heel. After all his bluster he was made of
very yielding clay indeed. e goes on:— With this
object in view, and for the sake of unity in the ranks
of Unionists, and as a meaning has been read into the
manifesto which was never intended by me, I unreser~
verly dissociate myself from amy portions_.of it which
have been construed as antagonistic to the settled
policy of the Unionist party in Ulster, and I repeat
what T have often stated in the Press and on the plat-
form, that I will continue to strenuously oppose Home
Ruile and any policy based on Lord Dunraven’s scheme
of Devolution in any shape or form. T will continue, as
I have always done, to' loyally co-operate with the
Ulster Unionist party at’ Westminster on all matters
relating to the maintenance of the Union and on the

vgeneral policy of the party. I fully adopt the pro-
gramme laid down in the resolutions proposed to be
submitted to the forthcoming meeting of the Ulster
Unionist Council.””  And thus this apostle of “the
wider nationality ’’ flops down once more into an
Orange Ulster dead head and bigot.

In the course of an article on “The Teaching of
Davis *’ in last week’s Zrish Screech, edited by Mr. Wm.
O’Brien, we read :— When we saw the representatives
of the young Independent Orangemen of Ulster the
other day appealing to their fellow-countrymen to shake
hands across the Boyne, to join in love and allegiance
to Ireland, and to work together so far as they could in
building up the prosperity of our common motherland,
we see again how the teaching of Davis is realising it-
self in practice, and is becoming one of the common-
places of the political creed of the Northerners as it has
iong been of the people of the South.” On the very
date appearing on that issue of Mr. O’Brien’s paper
Mr. T. Sloan, leader of these ¢ young Independent
Orangemen,’’ was writing his recantation !

We hope this incident will induce some of our green
people to rub their eyes. All that an Orangeman, or
one of England’s Faithful Garrison, has to do is utter
some vagde ‘mational aspiration, and the eyes of the
green people open, and they see wondrous visions of
Orange and Green standing shoulder to shoulder against
the common enemy, and their soft hearts melt, and
their backbones—such as they are—becume extra pliable.
Do you remember the “ Union of Hearts’’ with Eng-
land, dear reader? M. Sloan’s sudden recantation
will render people healthily sceptical of the recent
“Union of Hearts’’ that Mr. Sloan and his friends
were englneering.

A correspondent, in answer to the letter of “Curious’
in our last issue, informs us that Messrs. Collen Brothers
of Portadown, who got the contract from the Great Sick
and Sourface Railway, about which the public have
heard so much, are “saved.” It will be remembered
that it has been stated that this firm got the contract in
spite of a tender some £3,000 lower from Messrs. M‘Kee
and M‘Nally. Messrs. M‘Kee and M‘Nally are “ Idola-
tors.” Brother Goulding, “Grand Stewart’’ of the
notorious secret society, the Freemasons of Ireland, and
Chairman of the unhappy Great Sick and Sour Railway,
has refused to give any explanation to the shareholders
of the company for this extraordinary—or, perhaps,
ordinary—proceeding. We hear that a member of the
firm of Collen Bros. earned some fame on the occasion
of an excursion held by the Catholic Young Men’s As-
sociation in' August, 1900. The excursion was held on
a Sunday, a “ desecration ’’ that the “saved ’’ of Porta-
down could not allow. We hear that upwards of 5,000
“saved ”’ met the 600 or 700 ““Idolators’’ on their re-
turn. At a subsequent meeting of the Portadown
U.D.C. a member-of the firm of Collen Bros., in the
course of a discussion on the cost of extra police, stated
that it was a disgrace that a party of “ rebels’’ should
be allowed to walk down the streets of a “loyal”’ town
like Portadown.

We have another little item of “ intelligence’’ from
this “enlightened ”’ town from which Messrs. Collen
Bros. come from. In the Portadown News of Saturday,
December 23, 1905, we read in the report of the doings
of the Portadown Urban Council some news under the
sub-heading, “ Appointment, of Park-Keeper.’ The
wages attaching to this great office are 15s. a week.
Even so, the post attracted the attention of ten appli-
cants, one hailing from Scotland. One of the appli-

to relate, was an Idolator.”” About  six

mitted to have “ discharged the duties satisfactorily,

be found for him elsewhere. Mr. Blakley, Superinten-
dent of Works, said he had no vacancy for a_man at
present. It was eventually decided to give Harker a

There is only one “Idolator’’ on the Portadown
Council, and people who know Portadown intimately
will not, we believe, be much surprised that Harker,
after his six years’ satisfactory service, was dismissed
from this humble job at which he earned an honest, if
meagre, livelihood; indeed, the wonder is that he was

THE NEW YEER

HE year 1906 opens with a change of British Go-
vernment.  One kind of British Government
for Ireland may be a little less, or a little more, objec-
tionable to Ireland ; any sort of British Government for
the country means a serious impediment to national
and natural progress. The opening of the present year
marks a new phase in Irish development ; the Irish Ire-

land movement marked a new era.

The Irish Ireland movement has been developing
during the past six years or so in face of the British
Unionist Government; it has now to develop subject
to some extent to the British ““ Liberal ’ Government.
‘What Ireland has to do is to ““ get there,”’ as the Ame-
ricans say, and the path ought to be somewhat easier
with the British Liberal Government in power than it
was when a British Unionist Government held sway.

Ireland has need to push ahead with her masculine— .
as distinet from her raimeis—Sinn Fémn policy, the
while she uses the British political situation for all it is
worth.  Some people with a propensity for seeing
visions and tracing parallels even in such intricate
things as spiders’ webs may be laid aside at present as
negligible quantities; they don’t count. The floor of
the British House of Commons,’”’ to use our own now
oft-quoted phrase, is not the battle-ground of Irish
liberty, but it is a tactical position at present where
cffective work may possibly—nay, probably—be done
in the interests of this country.

Presuming that we have six years of “ Liberal ’’ Bri-
tish Government before us, those who, like ourselves,
stick our faith in Sinn Féin—always, of course, tem-
pered by reason and a sense of humour—have good
cause to be light and buoyant of heart. In face of the
fact that the late Unionist Lord Lieutenant called his
yacht by an Irish name, we may reasonably expect that
the present Liberal Government will at once take steps
to rescind the proposal to take away the extra fees for
the teaching of that language, which was laid under
contribution when the Unionist Lord Lieutenant was
searching for a name for his yacht.

Mr. William O’Brien is in a state of emotional erup-
tions—in some respects, but only in some, he reminds
us of our unconsciously comical old friend, George  the
saved ’—and he has given his photographs away with
his paper that contains the notorious diary. William

cvidently fancies himself a statesman. Well, the

country, we think we see that ideal in its true perspe
tive. Davis’s ideal did not work ; it fooled the “Id
tors,”” and the Sourfaces traded on it. We woul

“which the ex-Lord Chancellor was acting. It is high
ime that an Irishman should insist\'on knowing who
his accuser is. It would be useful to know what lumi-
mary is invested with the special prerogative of report-
ing the magistrates’ conduct.

The King’s Bench decided in the King v. Recorder
and Justices of Dublin in February, 1905, that in ac-
«cordance with Sec. 21 of the Licensing Act, 1874, the
Court shall declare as part of its sentence whether it
will, or will not, cause the conviction to be recorded on
the licence of a publican whom it convicts of a record-
able offence.

According to the Freeman of 19th December last, an
application was made the previous day in the King’s
Bench for an order of certiorari to bring up to be
quashed a conviction made at Ballybay Petty Sessions
on 27th November last against a publican for permitting
drunkenness on his licensed premises, on the ground
that the Court which fined the publican the trifling sum
of five shilljngs failed to state in its order whether the
said conviction was, or was not, to be recorded on the
licence of the publican.

Now, of course, this conviction cannot stand.  The
publican will spend £15 or £20 to have it cancelled.
It is cheaper, after all, to keep the police in good
humour than to get convictions quashed. Why did the
magistrates ignore the decision of the King’s Bench in
this case? There was no question of judicial discretion
here. There was no room for legitimate difference of
-opinion on the question involved. The magistrates
convicted and fined, but did not consider it worth their
while to make their order in conformity with the direc-
tion of the King’s Bench or Sec. 21 of the Licensing
Act of 1874.

Have these rebelly magistrates at Ballybay been re-
_ported to the ex-Lord Chancellor, and did he call upon
them to explain? Of course not, such grotesque proceed-
ings are reserved for matters connected with the national
ideals and aspirations of the Irish people. Though the
legislature, in its wisdom, considered permitting drun-
kenness twenty times as serious as a cart without a
name, and assigned the penalties accordingly of £10 and
10s., respectively. And such freaks are called law in
Ireland!

It is a question, all along the line, of Ascendancy
rversus all that remains of the Irish nation. And the
sooner the line of battle becomes more clearly ascer-
tained, and the conflict waxes warmer, so that the
many who presently try to stand partially under both
flags—that of the Garrison and that of the Irish nation

-—the sooner shall we see the beginning of the end of
Ascendancy, and I believe the shoneens and the
flunkeys are the greatest enemies to progress towards
this desirable consummation. CoLumBA.

confusion of thought, and whilst it may or may not be
polite to speak to Englishmen in this manner, for our-
selves it is best to understand what we are really seeking
and have a reasonable prospect of attaining. Now
local government and what the liberals propose to do,
be it much or little, are really two different things, and
the point of difference is Ulster. Gerald Balfour’s
Local Government Bill went as far as local government
strictly so-called could go; any proposed extemsion of
Irish autonomy must deal with the problem, not of
local but of central government, that is not of county

Now the stock objections to Irish self-government
have been, first, the fear of rebellion; secondly, the
. disturbance of the Imperial parliament—the retention
of Irish members and all that; and thirdly, the govern-
ment of the non-Irish half of Ulster against its will.
. None of these considerations affected the concession of
county government to any large extent; let us consider
their weight in regard to any scheme in the nature of
devolution or administrative Home Rule such as that
now foreshadowed. The bogey of rebellion will, of
course, have a certain weight, but it is probable that
this has an ever decreasing influence on the minds of
sonsible Englishmen. People who think of granting
‘responsible government to the Boers, and have already
concedéd a Transvaal 'parliament, can scarcely look
~ upon the Irish as more dangerous antagonists. Again,
disarrangement of the London parliament and of the
ited Kingdom generally, is evidently not likely to
sed by any scheme which creates no new parlia-

The importance of the point is this, that it shows
that a small measure, styled an extension of Local
Government, will raise almost as serious an outery in
Ireland as a very much larger measure which might be
called Home Rule. If the Irish Northern Protestant
knows his own interests, he will be more affected by a

equally likely to reject a measure of narrow as of

name of Home Rule seems to stink in British nostrils,

the thing, that the name, at any rate, may be avoided.

rather in the more practical and less showy field of
administration than in the gorgeous pageantry of par-

British elector, and therefore in administration they

article, the control of Irish affairs is mainly vested in

pray with the pupils was merely a direction and com-
mand of the law of the day.”

Finally, since there was no other University in Ire-
land except the University of Dublin, Erasmus Smith
was compelled to send the pupils of his schools to thal
_University, or to leave them without University educa-
tion altogether. The fact, then, that that University
was Puritan did not prove that Erasmus Smith had
Puritan intentions, any more than the fact of a gentle-
maun dining at a temperance hotel in a town in which
there is no other hotel would prove him to be a total
abstainer.

Moreover, by the Act of Transplantation passed on
the 26th of September, 1653, all Irish Catholics were to
remove into Connaught before the 1st of May, 1654,
under penalty of death, ““except husbandmen, plough-
men, labourers, -artificers, and others whose sole
property, real and personal, did not amount to £10.
Irish women married to English Protestants before the
2nd of December, 1650, provided they became Protes-
tants, those who could give proof of constant good
affection to the Parliament of England for 10 years, and
boys under 14 and girls under 12 in Protestant service,
and to be brought up as Protestants.’’

The word * Protestant’’ used here by Prendergast,
the author of the Cromwellian Settlement, means
“ Puritan;’’ for, as we have seen, no other Protestants
were tolerated, or allowed by Cromwell and his govern-
ment.

The latter words of this quotation prove that no
Catholic boys or girls were to beleft in any part of
Ireland, outside Connaught, except they were in Protes-
tant, 7.e., Puritan service, and being brought up Puri-
tans, 7.e., taught the Puritan Catechism and Puritan
prayers. If then “the laws of the day’ insisted so
strongly on the children in service being taught the
Puritan Catechism and Puritan prayers, how much more
strongly did they command, and insist, and compel, that
the Puritan Catechism and the Puritan prayers should
be taught to children in the schools? A fine of 2s. 6d.
was inflicted for not attending the Puritan church, on
Sunday.—Lingard, vol. viii., p. 178. The magistrates
were authorised to tender the oath of abjuration—
which abjured all the principal Catholic doctrines—to all
persons of the age of one and twenty, the refusal of
which subjected them to the loss of two-thirds of their
property and imprisonment during pleasure.

In 1642 2,500,000 acres were confiscated in the four
provinces of Ireland. By the Act of Settlement passed
on the 12th of August, 1652, all the rest of Ireland was
confiscated, except whatever parts were held by persons
who could give proof of constant good affection to the
Parliament of England for 10 years. That Act was the
prescription of the whole Irish nation. On the 26th of
September, 16563, a little Parliament called by Crom-
well and his officers, declared by. Act that all the ancient
estates and farms of Ireland belonged to the Adven-
turers and Army of England. Parliament set aside

- Connaught for the Irish, whither they must transplant
before the 1st of May, 1654, under,penalty of death.—

THE LEADER.

(Tbid.) It was the original design of the English
Parliament to transplant all the Irish Catholics into
Connaught, except those few classes exempted by the
Act of Transplantation which I have already men-
tioned.  Bub the Irish Tories had changed all that.
The Tories were Catholic proprietors wl:no I‘Oflﬁe‘-‘} to
transplant, took to the bogs and mountains and killed
the English planters wherever they got the opportunity.
They neither gave nor received quarter. Such was the
terror which they infused into the new settlers that in
some districts £200 were set on the heads of f:he leaders,
and £40 on the heads of the privates. EJ}gl@hmen who
had got estates in Ireland might risk their lives among
the Irish Tories, but English farmers and labourers
would not incur the risk of being shot by the Tories
for the doubtful benefit of an Irish farm. Hence, the
English settlers had to fall back on the Irish farmers,
or leave their lands derelict. And thus was prevented
the total extirpation of the Irish Catholics from three
provinces of Ireland. Black boys, and White boys, and
Peep o’Day boys, and all the others, who have warred
for the land since the time of Cromwell, have been the
successors of the Tories, and owe their existence to
English civilisation planted in Ireland by Cromwell.

The Parliament of England next resolved te divide
Treland into three districts—one of them to be a pure
Irish plantation, another a pure English plantation, in-
cluding foreign Protestants, 7.c., Puritans, and the
third a mixed plantation of English landlords and mas-
ters, having permission to take Irish tenants and ser-
vants, if such tenants and servants . were exempted from
the role of transplantation. Here again the master
should be English or Puritan.

Connaught, together with the County of Clare, had
been already assigned as the habitation of the Irish
nation ; with the exception of a short distance which
could be defended by forts, it formed an island.. A
margin of four miles round by the sea and river was to
be planted with English soldiers to completely hem in
the wild Hirish. The district between the Liffey and
the Barrow was assigned as the habitation of the pure
English plantation, and the rest of Ireland was for the
mixed plantation.

In the mixed plantation the children of the Irish
tenants and servants were “to be brought up under
English Protestant, ¢.e., Puritan, schoolmasters, and
they were to attend the public preaching of the Protes-
tant, 7.c., Puritan, ministers (Crom. Settl.; pp. 245-8).

The terror of the Tories again broke down the project
of the pure English plantation, and after the year
1656, the English planters were allowed to retain some
Irish tenants and servants (n very stringent conditions.
The proprietors were to engage that those tenants and
servants, whom they should be permitted to retain,
should become Protestants, 7.c., Puritans, within six
months ; that they should come to the meeting-house to
hear the word every Lord’s Day, if within four miles;
upon every other Lord’s Day if within six miles; if
further, once a month; and that their children should
learn the Catechism in the English tongue, without a
book, from the oral teaching of the ministers.—(Crom.
Settl., pp. 270-1). . g

 formances. As this statement is likely to leave a

gn dances were introduced at the afternoon per-

rom bsequent remarks that they were

It happened that my business comptj.llcd me to be:
present at the performances, both evening 'aud night,

peared. So far as I could see, there was no protest
raised against those exhibitions, on the contrary the:

wearing the badge, clapping hands and generally en-
couraging the repetition of a dance which would be more-
at home upon a music hall stage than at an Irish In-
dustrial Exhibition. I heard a young man, _who was
evidently a bit amazed, ask ome of the officials if a
certain dance (skirt) was really Irish, and, like many
other of our best productions, stolen from us by our
neighbours across the water. The reply given to thisi
enquiry was a cynical sneer of the pure “superior style,””
and adjusting his glasses, the official answered “ Now-a-
’tis not Irish.”

There were many other items produced which have
no connection whafever with this country, and it is to:
be hoped that those who were responsible for the
management, will take proper precautions in future to:
ensure that their programme is free from what is noth-
ing short of a travesty of the principles underlying the
movement which brought them into existence. It would
be well were dancers, vocalists, instrumentalists and the-
band compelled to submit the items which they in-
tended performing to those responsible, and thus avoid
any misgivings as to whose shoulders the blame (if any):
should rest upon.
